blocks.text: Strategy · Business Plan · Customer Journey OS · Agent Architecture · ChurnsOS · 10-Year Model
I design and build AI systems that run the full customer journey for SEA businesses — from first enquiry to repeat sale.

items.text: Identity and thesis: what Churns is, what it is not, and why SEA customer journeys are the wedge.
Business model: agency, vertical SaaS, education, and platform sequencing.
Customer Journey OS: GET, CONVERT, KEEP stages and audit flow.
Agent architecture: classifier, conversation, booking, objection, follow-up, reporting, and guard rails.
Milestones and KPIs: RM 10K to RM 1M MRR path, weekly dashboard, hiring triggers.

blocks.text: Churns AI is not a chatbot company. It is a Customer Journey Operating System company that uses AI agents as the delivery mechanism.

cells: Customer Journey Operating System using AI agents
Business outcomes company that uses technology
Agency that builds whatever clients ask for
Consultancy that audits the journey first, then builds the fix
Company that sells reply rates, conversion, staff hours saved, revenue recovered

items.text: The AI wrapper era is over; the surviving moat is integration depth into real customer journeys.
SEA has structural advantages: WhatsApp-first behaviour, local language complexity, fragmented payment rails, and business cultures Western SaaS does not map cleanly.
The real problem is not that owners do not know about AI. Nobody has helped them find the right starting point.
Bootstrap is right because agency cashflow funds vertical SaaS, and every client build becomes a paid product spec.

cells: Stop losing leads to slow WhatsApp replies and missed follow-ups.
Handle enquiries instantly, qualify serious buyers, and follow up consistently.
Handle DMs, comments, and post-stream follow-up at scale.
Run customer operations on AI before hiring ahead of cashflow.

blocks.text: Sequencing rule: Phase 1 funds Phase 2. Phase 2 funds Phase 3. Phase 3 builds the audience that makes Phase 4 possible. Right now: Phase 1 only.

cells: Customer Journey Audit -> build the AI system that fixes breaks
Package builds into Clinic OS, Creator OS, Property OS
Self-serve ChurnsOS: pick vertical, answer 10 questions, live in 48 hours

blocks.text: Every build maps the full customer journey from first message to repeat sale, then deploys AI agents to fix the breaks revealed by the audit.

items.text: Business basics: volume, channels, team size, urgency.
Stage 1 GET: reply speed, after-hours coverage, FAQ consistency.
Stage 2 CONVERT: follow-up frequency, nurture system, booking friction.
Stage 3 KEEP: post-sale process, complaints, reviews, reactivation.
The map is filled live and sent as the one-page output within 24 hours.

blocks.text: Three primary verticals, one research track. Never more than two primary at once.

cells: Same FAQs all day, booking calls backed up
Patients ghost after first session, no reactivation

items.text: Fastest path to accounts above RM 25K/mo.
Fixes DM/comment flood during streams, post-stream revenue bleed, product knowledge inconsistency, and multilingual audience gaps.
MCN compounding move: prove one creator, then sell the system across 30 creators.
Property is opportunistic until clinic retention is proven at RM 100K MRR.
Finance is the highest ceiling in SEA, but do not enter without a domain partner and compliance validation.

cells: Receives first message, logs contact, routes next agent
Assigns enquiry, booking, complaint, opt-out, urgent, unclear
Answers high-frequency questions from knowledge base
Handles price, trust, timing, and comparison objections
Confirms slots, captures details, prepares handoff
Finds cold customers and restarts the conversation
Turns Supabase events into monthly business reports

items.text: Never claim certainty where the business has not provided a rule.
Escalate urgent, medical, legal, refund, complaint, or high-risk cases.
Maintain customer memory, but do not repeat questions already answered.
All client-facing AI must log why it made a handoff or qualification decision.

blocks.text: ChurnsOS is not a future product. It is the organised, reusable form of everything built today.

Chatwoot and channel integration, staff accounts

Fix, tune prompts, improve logs, prepare first report

CHURNS AI — Content Intelligence Report v3
Platform stats · algorithm deep dives · what works · side-by-side voice comparison · 30 hooks
For business owners who are curious about AI but stuck on where to start.

items.text: Platform statistics overview
LinkedIn, X, Threads and Instagram algorithm notes
Cross-platform comparison
High-performing content patterns
Try-hard vs real voice comparison
30 hooks ready to adapt
Master cheat sheet

blocks.text: Four platforms. Different rules. Different audiences. Different formats that win. The biggest mistake is treating them the same.
One-line strategy: carousels for reach and saves, sharp text for conversation, native video for trust, links only in comments, reply to everything in the first hour.

LinkedIn uses separate ranking systems for Feed, Notifications and Search. Shares, saves, comments, depth of reading and comment quality now matter more than surface likes. A post with 5 genuine comments can outperform one with 50 likes.

cells: Post 1 carousel per week minimum. 6-8 slides, one idea per slide.
Native video only. YouTube links reduce reach.
Works when the observation is sharp. Longer thoughtful posts can win.
Never put links in the body. First comment only.
Make people describe their own situation in reply.
Make carousels useful enough to revisit.
Decision makers check before work starts.
Reply to every comment in the first hour.
Post from founder profile. Company page is secondary.

blocks.text: One-line strategy: short sharp observations daily, 1-2 threads per week, no links in posts, bookmarks are the metric that matters.
X ranks content by engagement velocity: how fast and how deeply people interact in the first few hours. Bookmarks are the strongest signal, followed by reply depth. Likes are weak.

cells: This is the platform for sharp observations.
Cut setup. Say the thing quickly.
Use for complex ideas 1-2 times per week.
Write observations and frameworks worth saving.
Reply chains are far more valuable than likes.
Worth considering if X becomes a serious channel.

blocks.text: One-line strategy: 2-3 posts per day. Short, opinionated, real. Add an image when you have one. Reply fast. Repeat the same topic cluster every week.
Threads is topic-based, not purely social-graph based. It learns the themes people engage with and routes posts into those topic clusters. Consistency teaches the algorithm what category you own.

cells: Early mover advantage is still open.
Add screenshots or simple graphics to your best posts.
Drive replies with a clear view.
Links work now, but images still usually win.
Never ask for likes, follows or shares.
Strong match for founders and operators.

blocks.text: Instagram is no longer one algorithm. Feed, Stories, Reels, Explore, Search and recommendations each rank differently. Views are now the shared performance metric across formats.

cells: Hook in the first 3 seconds. Every second must earn the next.
Make content people send to a friend.
Useful with existing followers, weaker for discovery.
Genuine comments matter more than emoji comments.
Make viewers tap and find exactly what they just liked.
Relationship, past engagement, quick interaction and profile visit rate
Use carousels and founder observations that make people tap profile.
Completion, rewatch, DM share, follow after viewing
Show the result first, then explain. No intro.
3-5 per day for warm audience relationship.
Strong prior surface performance, 95% completion, linger time, follows
Start at the most interesting moment.
Make it useful to send to a friend.
Use proof, numbers, or visual walkthroughs.
Use trending audio only when natural.
Add your own perspective and voice.
Link in bio or first comment only.
AI content with no human layer
Easy for audience and algorithm to ignore
Draft with AI, rewrite in your voice.

blocks.text: Keywords in captions, on-screen text, profile and account history now matter more than hashtags. For Churns, captions should contain terms like AI, clinic, WhatsApp, automation, receptionist and Malaysia. Use 3-5 tightly relevant hashtags, not 20-30 generic tags.

cells: 3x more dwell time than single image
Save this if you want to see how it works.
Show the most interesting part first
Save this. Full breakdown in DMs.
Save and share with someone who needs it.
DM me if you want this for your business.

blocks.text: Reels bring them in. Carousels make them stay. Stories keep them warm.

cells: States something the reader has felt but not heard said
The hardest part of AI is not learning it. It is knowing where to point it first.
Real numbers from a real situation
23 messages. 4 bookings. 0 human replies. One Saturday night.
AI is not going to take your job. Your competitor who uses it might.
Something went wrong or surprised you
The AI gave a patient wrong pricing. Here is what changed.
One idea per slide, designed to revisit
You tried AI. It felt like a toy. Here is why.
Here is the difference between a chatbot and a vertical AI operating system and why that changes everything.
People keep calling what I build a chatbot. It is not a chatbot. A chatbot waits to be asked. What I build goes and gets the booking.
A clinic in KL was losing 40% of their WhatsApp enquiries. Here is what we found when we mapped the data.
A clinic was losing 40% of its evening enquiries. Not bad service. Just nobody there to reply. Fixed it.
Revenue leak audit I ran for a client: 4 specific points where the business was losing leads.
There are 4 moments in every service business where a warm lead quietly disappears and nobody notices until the end of the month.
Running two companies using AI as the operating infrastructure. Here is the honest breakdown.
Two companies. No team. AI does the ops. I do the decisions. Weirdly manageable.
OpenAI launched a $4B company. Here is my complete take on what it means for business owners in KL.
OpenAI raised $4B to work with Fortune 500s. The clinic owner in PJ is not in that conversation. That gap is where I build.
The follow-up problem that kills 60% of warm leads. The data says 4-7 touches are needed.
Most businesses follow up once then call the lead cold. The lead was never cold. You just stopped showing up.
I started ChurnsOS this week. Here is why it is the most important thing I built.
Started a folder where every system I build goes. By client 10 I will be reusing 80% of it. The folder is the product.

blocks.text: Rule: try-hard announces what is coming. Real voice just says the thing. Cut the last sentence. It is almost always where you explain what the post meant.

items.text: The hardest part of AI is not learning it. It is knowing where to point it first.
Everyone tried ChatGPT. Most people stopped there. The value starts one step after that.
You tried it. It felt like a toy. That is not a you problem. Nobody showed you where to aim.
Hot take: most business owners do not need more AI tools. They need someone to help them pick one and go deep.
The gap between dabbling with AI and actually getting value is not knowledge. It is specificity.
[Number] messages. [Number] bookings. Zero human replies. One [day].
XALT got [X] customer messages last week. I handled [Y]. The business ran anyway.
Before: 40% of evening enquiries missed. After: 3%. Same business. Same team. Different system.
Hired an AI employee. RM 200/month. Never sick. Never late. Never misses a follow-up.
Client message this week: "I stopped checking my phone at night." That is the product.
AI is not going to take your job. Your competitor who uses it might. Different conversation.
AI did not replace my staff. It meant I never needed to hire them in the first place.
Most people use AI like a search engine. The ones getting results use it like a new hire.
Running lean is not about doing less. It is about making sure the right things still get done.
The problem is not that people do not understand AI. Nobody helped them find the right problem.
The AI gave a client wrong information. I got the angry message at 9pm. Here is what I did and what I changed.
Thought the hardest part of building AI for a business would be the AI. It was getting the client to write down their own process.
Week where nothing clicked. Posted anyway. That is the whole job.
What I know now that I did not know 60 days ago about posting online.
Month 1 of building in public. Expected to attract clients. Also attracted people who just wanted to talk. Both are useful.
POV: you finally try AI properly and realise you have been doing something manually for 2 years that takes 10 minutes.
Two companies. No team. AI does the inbox, follow-up, reports. I do the decisions. Weirdly manageable.
The peace of mind AI gives you is underrated. Knowing the inbox is handled while you sleep is worth more than the cost.
OpenAI raised $4B to work with Fortune 500s. The clinic owner in PJ is not in that conversation. That gap is where I build.
Most AI content talks about what AI can do. Nobody talks about what it cannot do yet. That gap is more useful.
If AI still feels like a toy to you, drop STUCK in my DMs. I will show you where it fits in 30 minutes.
Every business owner I talk to says the same thing: I know I should use AI but I do not know where to start. That conversation is one I can help with.
Not for developers. Not for tech teams. If you run a real business and AI has not clicked yet, that is exactly who I build for.
The moment AI clicks is different for every business. It is usually the day you realise you were doing something manually that did not need to be.
If this resonates, you are probably the person I am talking to. Say hi.

blocks.text: How to start · day by day · platform by platform · what to post
Posting starts Sunday 17 May. Audience: business owners who are curious about AI but stuck on where to start.
Before writing any post, ask: would I say this to a friend at mamak who runs a business? If yes, post it exactly like that. If no, rewrite until you would.

cells: Record 15-20 min voice note or Loom: what you built, what surprised you, what a client said, what you noticed.
Feed transcript to Claude and extract 5 post ideas: observation, use case, honest moment, reframe, entrepreneur take.
Write all posts and schedule LinkedIn, Threads, X, and Instagram.
Record 1 IG Reel: face to camera or screen recording with narration and captions.
Reply to every comment and DM within first hour. Move hot leads personally.
Review which posts drove DMs, comments, saves. Double down next week.
One true thing. No setup. No lesson.
One real scenario: what happened, what AI did, what changed.
6-8 slides, one idea all the way, designed to be saved.
Something went wrong or surprised you. Vulnerability without victimhood.

Never rely on one AI. Build a rotation.

cells: The businesses winning with AI right now are not the most technical ones. They are the most specific ones.

items.text: M1: zero paying clients by end of June -> stop content and run 10 more calls.
M2: zero DMs after 30 posts -> posts are too educational; make them more observational.
M3: delivery still takes 14 days -> stop new clients and template existing builds first.
M4: below RM 70K MRR by end of Q4 -> freeze product work, revisit offer and pricing.
M6: self-serve below 50 signups in 60 days -> return to assisted onboarding.

cells: Own delivery, frees founder to sell/create

blocks.text: Strategic Master Plan 2026 to 9-Figure Exit · Updated May 2026

items.text: Brand DNA and positioning define what XALT should feel like in every channel.
Product, finance, distribution, marketing, AI systems, KPI, hiring, milestone, and exit pages hold the operating plan.
The pre-launch checklist is split by execution phase so the team can track tasks directly inside this workspace.

blocks.text: To become Southeast Asia's most trusted everyday hydration brand — the one people reach for without thinking twice, because it actually works and feels like it was made for their real life.

XALT makes functional hydration effortless, honest, and worth sharing. We create clean, effective electrolyte products for people who show up daily — at their desk, in the gym, on the move — and need to feel sharp, not stimulated.

cells: Vibrant, approachable, honest, science-backed. Poppi energy meets functional wellness.
No nasties. No sugar crash. No confusion. Just hydration that works.
Calm clarity. Like you've got your life together, quietly.
Clean, modern, desk-chic. Something you're proud to be seen with.
The Strava log of hydration. A daily ritual you post about.
Core brand and daily habit positioning
Hydration that works for the everyday you.
The one thing you'll actually do every day.
Made for people who show up, not show off.

items.text: Consistency beats intensity in hydration and in business.
Transparency first: build in public and share wins and losses with the community.
Ingredient honesty: no hidden nasties, ever.
Community over customers: build tribes, not transactions.
Earn before you scale: cashflow funds growth.
Sweat the details on flavour because great taste drives repeat purchase.
Add sugar or artificial stimulants to hit a sales metric.
Overstate efficacy claims without scientific backing.
Partner with creators who do not authentically align with the brand.
Scale before the product experience is right.

cells: Corporate desk, entrepreneur, salesperson, content creator, university student
Office 9-5, gym/yoga/pilates 3x per week, travel 1-2x per month, side hustle evenings
3pm brain fog, constant bathroom trips from plain water, jet lag, feeling puffy not sharp
I need to stay hydrated and focused all day. Something desk-chic, not Gatorade.
Morning desk ritual, pre/post workout, airport lounge, post-run club share
Hardcore CrossFit, stimulant seekers, sedentary non-actives

items.text: Active teens 13-18: school, tuition, weekend sports, dry skin, headachy afternoons, and a shareable product identity.
Men 18-35 functional minimalists: work, gym, social, better habits, no gimmicks, no loud sports-drink vibe.

blocks.text: Positioning: Daily hydration with the perfect amount of electrolytes for the everyday you. Calm focus plus adequate sweat replacement. Zero sugar. 3 essential vitamins, 6 key electrolytes, clean ingredients.

cells: Na 500mg, K 300mg, Mg 100mg, Ca, Phosphorus, Chloride
Vitamin B6 for focus, Vitamin D for bones/immune, Vitamin C antioxidant

blocks.text: Use this as the working execution board. Add due dates to tasks so the Tasks by Due Date tab can group them properly.
XALT founder-led content scripts for Week 1. Keep the reel clean, premium, personal, and honest.

items.text: Episode 1 — The Real Reason We Started XALT (45s Founder Reel)

blocks.text: Use subtitles only for the exact words spoken. Do not add extra callouts, explanatory captions, or clutter. The reel should feel like a founder story, not an ad.
Creative direction: Keep the reel clean and premium. No extra on-screen text. Only use subtitles that match exactly what is spoken in the hook, voiceover, and CTA.
Open a timer while recording. 45 seconds total. Read this aloud during takes.
"A few months ago, I was admitted to the hospital for kidney stones."
"Not once — four times in two years."
"Same diagnosis every time: I wasn’t hydrating properly."
"So we did what any reasonable person would do…"
"Turns out, humans don’t just hydrate with water."
"So we started looking for something better."
"But every drink we picked up felt the same."
"And the ones we did understand were usually just sugar."
"Other drinks we found on the shelf felt too intense, too clinical, too serious."
"Like they were made for someone finishing an Ironman at 5am."
"Working, running errands, going gym, and trying not to crash by 3pm."
"An electrolyte drink made for everyday hydration with obsessive flavours."
"Something you’d actually look forward to daily — like your morning coffee."
Female: "We’re documenting every step of this journey."
Shoot everything vertical in 9:16. iPhone back camera. 4K — 24fps or 30fps. Keep B-roll clips short: 2–4 seconds each.

Record with the wireless mic

blocks.text: Use subtitles only for the exact words spoken. Do not add extra text labels, callouts, or explanatory captions.

items.text: Do NOT add: "4 hospital visits", "Too much sugar", "Everyday hydration", "Follow the build", or "Made for 3PM crash"
Use white subtitle, clean font, medium size, bottom center
Add slight black shadow or translucent background for readability
Only highlight key words if needed: kidney stones, four times, Chat, Dipotassium phosphate, Sucralose, sugar, XALT
Do not overdo highlights — the reel should feel clean and premium
Record the hook and CTA with the wireless mic directly into the iPhone
Record the rest as a clean voiceover after filming — this keeps visuals moving fast while story stays clear
Shoot everything vertical in 9:16
Use the iPhone back camera when possible for better quality
Use 4K — either 24fps or 30fps
Keep most B-roll clips short: 2–4 seconds each
Final feeling: real problem → personal reason → market frustration → why XALT exists

blocks.text: Write the caption AFTER reviewing the final cut. Match the tone: honest, warm, founder-led. No hype language.
Here’s the real reason we started XALT.
A few months ago, I ended up in the hospital for kidney stones. Not once — four times in two years. Same answer every time: not hydrating properly.
So we went looking for something better. What we found on every shelf felt the same — ingredients we couldn’t pronounce, too much sugar, or drinks built for elite athletes who wake up at 5am.
We’re everyday people. Working, running errands, going to the gym, and trying not to crash by 3pm.
So we stopped looking. And started building XALT.
An electrolyte drink made for everyday hydration — with obsessive flavours you’d actually look forward to.
We’re documenting every step of this journey. Follow along.

items.text: Keep line breaks — Instagram rewards readability, and short lines stop the scroll
First line is the hook — must match or riff off what’s said in the video
CTA at the end: "Follow along" is soft and honest — do not add "link in bio" unless there’s a real destination
Hashtags: 5–10 relevant ones, not spammy — mix brand + niche + broad
Review caption against final cut before posting — the story must feel consistent
